steps.instruction: Place tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound.
steps.helpDetail: Never place on a joint. Place on bare skin if possible. If wound is on a limb, apply tourniquet between wound and heart.

steps.instruction: Pull strap tight and secure it.
steps.helpDetail: The strap should be tight enough that you cannot slide a finger underneath. Pain is expected and acceptable.

steps.instruction: Twist the windlass rod until bleeding stops.
steps.helpDetail: Turn clockwise. It will hurt. Do NOT stop until the bleeding has fully stopped. Usually 3-4 turns.

steps.instruction: Lock the windlass in place and note the time.
steps.helpDetail: Write the time on the patient's forehead with marker or blood. Write 'TQ' and the time. Do NOT remove until hospital care.

steps.instruction: Apply direct pressure with a clean cloth.
steps.helpDetail: Use gauze, a t-shirt, or any clean fabric. Press firmly. Do not lift to check — this disrupts clotting.

steps.instruction: Elevate the wound above the heart if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Gravity reduces blood flow to the wound. Raise the limb while maintaining pressure.

steps.instruction: Wrap with elastic bandage snugly but not cutting off circulation.
steps.helpDetail: Check fingers/toes below the bandage for warmth, color, and feeling every 15 minutes.

steps.instruction: If blood soaks through, add more material on top.
steps.helpDetail: Never remove the first layer. Stack additional padding and re-wrap. Removing disrupts the clot.

techniques.name: Wound Irrigation & Cleaning
techniques.subtitle: Prevent infection in cuts and scrapes

steps.instruction: Wash your hands thoroughly before touching the wound.
steps.helpDetail: Dirty hands introduce bacteria. Use soap and water, or hand sanitizer if available.

steps.instruction: Flush the wound with clean running water for 5 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Use a squeeze bottle, hydration bladder, or water bottle with a pinhole in the cap to create pressure. Pressure irrigation is more effective than soaking.

steps.instruction: Remove visible debris with clean tweezers.
steps.helpDetail: Remove dirt, gravel, splinters. Do NOT remove deeply embedded objects — stabilize them in place.

steps.instruction: Apply antibiotic ointment and cover with a clean bandage.
steps.helpDetail: Change the dressing daily. Watch for signs of infection: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus, or red streaks.

steps.instruction: Clean and dry the wound edges thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: Strips won't adhere to wet or dirty skin. Pat dry the skin around the wound completely.

steps.instruction: Apply tincture of benzoin to skin on both sides of the wound.
steps.helpDetail: This makes the adhesive stick much better. Let it get tacky (30 seconds) before applying strips.

steps.instruction: Apply strips perpendicular to the wound, pulling edges together.
steps.helpDetail: Start at the center, then alternate sides. Space strips 3mm apart. Don't overlap them.

steps.instruction: Apply reinforcing strips parallel along the strip ends.
steps.helpDetail: These anchor strips prevent the closure strips from peeling off. Cover with a non-stick bandage.

steps.instruction: Check responsiveness. Tap shoulders and shout.
steps.helpDetail: If no response: Call for help. If alone, do 2 minutes of CPR before leaving to get help.

steps.instruction: Place heel of hand on center of chest, between nipples.
steps.helpDetail: Interlock your other hand on top. Lock your elbows. Position shoulders directly over your hands.

steps.instruction: Compress chest 2 inches deep at 100-120 beats per minute.
steps.helpDetail: Push hard and fast. Allow full chest recoil between compressions. Think of the beat of 'Stayin' Alive' by the Bee Gees.

steps.instruction: Give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions.
steps.helpDetail: Tilt head back, lift chin, pinch nose, seal mouth. Each breath should last 1 second. Watch for chest rise.

steps.instruction: Continue 30:2 cycle without stopping until help arrives.
steps.helpDetail: Switch rescuers every 2 minutes if possible. Fatigue degrades compression quality rapidly.

steps.instruction: Confirm choking — person cannot speak, cough, or breathe.
steps.helpDetail: Universal sign is hands clutching throat. Ask 'Are you choking?' If they can speak or cough forcefully, encourage them to keep coughing.

steps.instruction: Stand behind the person and wrap arms around their waist.
steps.helpDetail: For a pregnant person or very large person, place hands higher on the chest instead.

steps.instruction: Make a fist with one hand, place thumb side against abdomen above navel.
steps.helpDetail: Well below the ribcage, well above the navel. This is the soft area of the upper abdomen.

steps.instruction: Thrust inward and upward with quick, forceful movements.
steps.helpDetail: Each thrust should be a separate, distinct movement. Repeat until the object is expelled or person becomes unconscious.

steps.instruction: Recognize shock: pale/cool/clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, confusion.
steps.helpDetail: Other signs: rapid shallow breathing, nausea, thirst, dilated pupils, blue lips.

steps.instruction: Lay the person flat and elevate legs 8-12 inches.
steps.helpDetail: This drives blood to vital organs. Do NOT elevate legs if you suspect spine, head, or leg injuries.
steps.illustrationDescription: Person lying flat with elevated legs.

steps.instruction: Maintain body temperature — cover with blankets or coats.
steps.helpDetail: Cover top AND bottom. Insulate from the ground. Shock patients lose body heat rapidly.

steps.instruction: Do NOT give food or water. Moisten lips only.
steps.helpDetail: They may need surgery. Anything in the stomach complicates anesthesia. Moisten lips with a wet cloth.

steps.instruction: Kneel beside the person. Extend near arm at right angle.
steps.helpDetail: Palm facing up. This prevents them from rolling onto their face.

steps.instruction: Place far hand against their near cheek, hold it there.
steps.helpDetail: Back of their hand against their cheek. Keep holding it in place.

steps.instruction: Pull the far knee up with your other hand.
steps.helpDetail: Foot should be flat on the ground. This creates a pivot point for rolling.

steps.instruction: Roll them toward you by pulling the raised knee.
steps.helpDetail: They should end up on their side. Tilt head back slightly to keep airway open. Their own hand cushions their head.

techniques.name: Arm/Wrist Splint
techniques.subtitle: Immobilize a broken arm or wrist

steps.instruction: Do NOT attempt to realign the bone.
steps.helpDetail: Immobilize in the position found. Moving a fracture can sever blood vessels and nerves.

steps.instruction: Find a rigid splint material: stick, cardboard, rolled magazine.
steps.helpDetail: Splint must be long enough to immobilize joints above and below the break.

steps.instruction: Pad the splint with cloth and secure with bandages or strips.
steps.helpDetail: Tie at minimum 3 points: above break, below break, and at each adjacent joint. Don't tie directly over the fracture.

steps.instruction: Check circulation below the splint: warmth, color, feeling, pulse.
steps.helpDetail: Check every 15-20 minutes. Loosen if fingers turn blue, become numb, or swell significantly.

steps.instruction: Immobilize the leg in the position found.
steps.helpDetail: If the foot is rotated or the leg is bent at an unnatural angle, do NOT straighten it. Splint as-is.

steps.instruction: Use two long rigid objects as splints — one inside, one outside the leg.
steps.helpDetail: Walking sticks, branches, tent poles, rolled sleeping pads. Inner splint from groin to foot. Outer from armpit to foot for femur breaks.

steps.instruction: Pad between the leg and splints generously.
steps.helpDetail: Use clothing, towels, or moss. Fill all gaps. Pressure points cause tissue damage over time.

steps.instruction: Secure with wide bandages at 4-5 points. Avoid tying over the break.
steps.helpDetail: Tie at ankle, below knee, above knee, and hip. For femur fractures, include a figure-8 at the foot.

steps.instruction: Fold a square cloth into a triangle.
steps.helpDetail: A bandana, shirt, or any ~40-inch square of fabric works. Cut diagonally if needed.

steps.instruction: Slide the triangle under the injured arm and behind the elbow.
steps.helpDetail: One point at the elbow, one over the shoulder on the injured side, the other over the opposite shoulder.

steps.instruction: Tie the two upper points behind the neck.
steps.helpDetail: The knot should sit to the side of the neck, not directly on the spine. Pad the knot if needed.

steps.instruction: Pin or tie the elbow point to secure it.
steps.helpDetail: The forearm should be level or slightly elevated. Fingers should be visible for circulation checks.

steps.instruction: Suspect spinal injury: any fall > 3 feet, vehicle accident, diving, or head trauma.
steps.helpDetail: If unsure, ALWAYS assume spinal injury. The cost of being cautious is zero. The cost of being wrong is paralysis.

steps.instruction: Tell the patient to NOT MOVE. Hold their head in the exact position found.
steps.helpDetail: Place your hands on both sides of their head. Keep head, neck, and spine in alignment. Do not let anyone move them.

steps.instruction: Place rolled towels or clothing along both sides of the neck.
steps.helpDetail: These prevent rotation. Tape or wrap across the forehead and padding to stabilize. Improvise a cervical collar.

steps.instruction: Only move with log-roll technique if absolutely necessary.
steps.helpDetail: Requires 3+ people. One holds head, others roll body as a single unit. Only move if staying in place is life-threatening (fire, flood).

steps.instruction: Remove from heat source. Remove clothing/jewelry from burn area.
steps.helpDetail: If clothing is stuck to the burn, cut around it. Do NOT pull stuck clothing off. Remove watches and rings before swelling.

steps.instruction: Cool the burn with cool (not cold) running water for 20 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT use ice, butter, toothpaste, or any home remedy. Cool water only. 20 minutes is the evidence-based number.

steps.instruction: Cover with cling wrap or a clean non-fluffy dressing.
steps.helpDetail: Lay cling wrap over the burn — do not wrap tightly. This keeps it clean and reduces pain from air exposure.

steps.instruction: Do NOT pop blisters. They are sterile bandages.
steps.helpDetail: Intact blisters protect healing skin underneath. If a blister breaks on its own, gently clean and cover.

steps.instruction: Brush off dry chemicals before adding water.
steps.helpDetail: Some dry chemicals react with water. Brush away from the body first, then flush.

steps.instruction: Flush with large amounts of water for at least 20 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Use a garden hose, shower, or any source of running water. More is better. Chemical burns penetrate deeper over time.

steps.instruction: Remove contaminated clothing while flushing.
steps.helpDetail: Clothing traps chemicals against skin. Cut clothing off if needed. Protect your own hands.

steps.instruction: Do NOT try to neutralize the chemical.
steps.helpDetail: Neutralization reactions generate heat, making the burn worse. Water dilution is always the correct answer.

steps.instruction: Move away from the snake. Do NOT try to catch or kill it.
steps.helpDetail: Note the snake's appearance for identification if possible. A photo from a safe distance helps. Most bites happen during the second encounter.

steps.instruction: Keep the bitten limb below heart level and immobilize it.
steps.helpDetail: Remove watches, rings, tight clothing. Swelling will be rapid. Splint the limb to reduce venom spread.

steps.instruction: Do NOT cut, suck, tourniquet, or apply ice to the bite.
steps.helpDetail: All of these cause additional damage and do not remove venom. These are myths that worsen outcomes.
steps.illustrationDescription: Warning: do not cut or suck.

steps.instruction: Mark the edge of swelling and note the time.
steps.helpDetail: Use a pen to outline swelling every 15 minutes. This helps medical staff judge venom severity and progression.

steps.instruction: Evacuate to a hospital. Antivenom is the only treatment.
steps.helpDetail: Carry the victim if possible — walking increases venom circulation. Time to antivenom is the most important factor.

steps.instruction: Grasp the tick as close to the skin as possible with fine-tipped tweezers.
steps.helpDetail: Grab the head/mouthparts, NOT the body. Squeezing the body can inject pathogens (Lyme, RMSF) into the wound.

steps.instruction: Pull straight up with steady, even pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Do not twist, jerk, or rock. Steady tension. The tick will release in 30-60 seconds. If mouthparts break off, leave them — they'll work out naturally.

steps.instruction: Clean the bite area with soap and water or alcohol.
steps.helpDetail: Also clean your hands and the tweezers. Apply antibiotic ointment.

steps.instruction: Monitor for 30 days for a bull's-eye rash or flu symptoms.
steps.helpDetail: Expanding red ring = possible Lyme disease. Seek medical care immediately if symptoms appear.

steps.instruction: Scrape the stinger out sideways with a flat edge.
steps.helpDetail: Use a credit card, knife blade, or fingernail. Do NOT squeeze with tweezers — this injects more venom.

steps.instruction: Clean with soap and water. Apply cold pack for 15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Wrap ice in cloth — never direct ice on skin. This reduces swelling and pain.

steps.instruction: Watch for anaphylaxis: facial swelling, difficulty breathing, hives.
steps.helpDetail: Anaphylaxis can start within minutes. If the person has an EpiPen, help them use it immediately.

steps.instruction: For mild reactions: antihistamine and pain reliever.
steps.helpDetail: Benadryl for itching/swelling, ibuprofen for pain. Keep the area clean and monitor for infection.

steps.instruction: Recognize: throat tightening, facial swelling, hives, wheezing, dizziness.
steps.helpDetail: Anaphylaxis is life-threatening. It can progress from mild to fatal in minutes. Act immediately.

steps.instruction: Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) into outer thigh.
steps.helpDetail: Push firmly through clothing if needed. Hold for 10 seconds. Massage the site for 10 seconds after. Can be given through jeans.

steps.instruction: Call emergency services immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Even after epinephrine, the person needs hospital monitoring. Anaphylaxis can return (biphasic reaction) hours later.

steps.instruction: Place in position of comfort. Usually sitting up for breathing.
steps.helpDetail: If feeling faint, lay down with legs elevated. If vomiting or unconscious, recovery position. A second EpiPen dose can be given after 5 minutes if no improvement.

steps.instruction: Get the person out of the cold and remove wet clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Replace wet clothes with dry layers. If no dry clothes, use any insulating material — newspaper, leaves, garbage bags.

steps.instruction: Insulate from the ground and cover with multiple layers.
steps.helpDetail: Most heat is lost to the ground. Place sleeping pads, backpacks, branches underneath. Wrap in blankets, sleeping bags, or vapor barrier.

steps.instruction: Apply warm (not hot) packs to core areas: neck, armpits, groin.
steps.helpDetail: Use warm water bottles, heated rocks wrapped in cloth, or warm-water-filled zip bags. Never apply heat to extremities first — this causes fatal rewarming shock.

steps.instruction: Give warm sweet drinks if conscious and alert.
steps.helpDetail: Warm water with sugar or honey. No alcohol (it dilates blood vessels and increases heat loss). No caffeine.

steps.instruction: Handle gently — a severely hypothermic heart is fragile.
steps.helpDetail: Rough handling can trigger cardiac arrest in severe hypothermia. Move slowly and gently. Do not rub extremities.

techniques.name: Heat Stroke Treatment
techniques.subtitle: Cool rapidly — this is a medical emergency

steps.instruction: Recognize: hot/red/dry skin, confusion, temp over 104°F, loss of consciousness.
steps.helpDetail: Heat stroke is NOT heat exhaustion. If they've stopped sweating and are confused/unconscious, this is a dire emergency.

steps.instruction: Move to shade. Remove excess clothing.
steps.helpDetail: Get them out of the sun immediately. Strip to underwear if possible.

steps.instruction: Cool aggressively: wet the entire body and fan.
steps.helpDetail: Evaporative cooling is most effective. Douse with water and fan continuously. Pack ice in armpits, neck, and groin. Immerse in cool water if a stream or pool is nearby.

steps.instruction: Evacuate immediately. This is a 911 call.
steps.helpDetail: Untreated heat stroke has a 70%+ mortality rate. Brain damage starts quickly. No home remedy suffices.

steps.instruction: Recognize: headache, nausea, dizziness, fatigue above 8,000 ft.
steps.helpDetail: AMS (Acute Mountain Sickness) affects everyone differently. Past performance doesn't predict future occurrence.

steps.instruction: Stop ascending. Rest at current altitude.
steps.helpDetail: If symptoms are mild: rest, hydrate, eat carbs, take ibuprofen. If symptoms are moderate: descend.

steps.instruction: If symptoms worsen or don't improve in 24 hours: descend 1,000+ feet.
steps.helpDetail: HACE (high altitude cerebral edema): confusion, loss of coordination. HAPE (high altitude pulmonary edema): cough with pink frothy sputum. Both are fatal without descent.

steps.instruction: Emergency: give supplemental oxygen if available. Descend immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Gamow bags simulate lower altitude. Dexamethasone for HACE, nifedipine for HAPE — but descent is the definitive treatment.

steps.instruction: Recognize: dark urine, dry mouth, headache, dizziness, rapid heart rate.
steps.helpDetail: Pinch test: pinch skin on back of hand — if it stays tented for more than 2 seconds, dehydration is significant.

steps.instruction: Give oral rehydration solution: water + salt + sugar.
steps.helpDetail: Recipe: 1 liter clean water + 6 teaspoons sugar + 1/2 teaspoon salt. Sip slowly over hours. Too fast causes vomiting.

steps.instruction: Small frequent sips — do not gulp.
steps.helpDetail: 1-2 tablespoons every 5 minutes for severely dehydrated patients. Increase as tolerated. Vomiting worsens dehydration.

steps.instruction: Rest in shade and minimize activity until rehydrated.
steps.helpDetail: Monitor urine output and color. Goal is clear to light yellow. Resume activity only when urine is light-colored.

steps.instruction: Do NOT rub the eye.
steps.helpDetail: Rubbing drives foreign material deeper and can scratch the cornea permanently. Fight the instinct.

steps.instruction: For small particles: flush with clean water for 15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Pull upper lid over lower lid to generate tears. Flush from inner corner outward. Use an eyecup or water-filled container.

steps.instruction: For impaled objects: DO NOT REMOVE. Stabilize in place.
steps.helpDetail: Tape a cup or cone over the eye to protect the object. Cover BOTH eyes — eye movement is synchronized, covering one reduces movement of the other.

steps.instruction: For chemical splash: flush continuously for 20+ minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Hold eye open and irrigate. Flush from inner to outer corner. Continue even if they resist — saving the eye is worth the discomfort.

techniques.name: Dental Emergency
techniques.subtitle: Save a knocked-out tooth within 30 min

steps.instruction: Pick up the tooth by the crown (white part), NEVER the root.
steps.helpDetail: The root has living cells that must survive. Touching the root kills cells. Handle only the crown.

steps.instruction: Rinse gently with milk or saliva. Do NOT scrub.
steps.helpDetail: If milk is available, use it. If not, have the patient spit into a cup and rinse the tooth in their saliva.

steps.instruction: Attempt to reinsert into the socket.
steps.helpDetail: Push firmly in and have the patient bite down on a cloth to hold it. If unable to reinsert, keep in milk or inside the patient's cheek.

steps.instruction: Get to a dentist within 30 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Every minute counts. Success rate drops from 90% at 5 minutes to 5% at 60 minutes. Time is teeth.

steps.instruction: At FIRST sign of a hot spot: STOP and address it.
steps.helpDetail: A hot spot takes 2 minutes to fix. A blister can take you out of action for days. Never push through friction.

steps.instruction: Apply moleskin or athletic tape over the hot spot.
steps.helpDetail: Cut a donut shape — hole over the hot spot, padding around it. This eliminates friction. Duct tape works as a last resort.

steps.instruction: If a blister has formed, do NOT pop small blisters.
steps.helpDetail: The fluid is sterile and promotes healing. Cover with padding and reduce friction. Small = smaller than a dime.

steps.instruction: For large blisters that impede walking: drain from the edge.
steps.helpDetail: Sterilize a needle with fire or alcohol. Puncture at the edge. Press fluid out. Do NOT remove the skin roof — it's a natural bandage. Apply antibiotic and cover.

steps.instruction: Apply RICE: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation.
steps.helpDetail: RICE within the first hour dramatically reduces swelling and recovery time.

steps.instruction: Start wrapping at the ball of the foot.
steps.helpDetail: Use an elastic bandage. Two wraps around the ball of the foot to anchor.

steps.instruction: Wrap in a figure-8 pattern around the ankle.
steps.helpDetail: Go around the arch, up over the ankle, cross over the front, and repeat. Each layer should overlap the previous by half.

steps.instruction: Secure the bandage. Check toe circulation.
steps.helpDetail: Toes should remain pink and warm. You should be able to wiggle them. If not, the wrap is too tight — loosen immediately.

steps.instruction: Remove victim from water using a reaching aid.
steps.helpDetail: Extend a pole, rope, or branch. Never swim to a drowning person unless trained—they will grab and pull you under.

steps.instruction: Lay victim flat on back. Check for breathing.
steps.helpDetail: Look, listen, feel for 10 seconds. Drowning victims often have water blocking airways.

steps.instruction: If not breathing: give 5 rescue breaths first.
steps.helpDetail: Drowning protocol differs from normal CPR—start with breaths. Pinch nose, seal mouth, blow until chest rises.

steps.instruction: Begin CPR: 30 compressions, 2 breaths. Continue until breathing resumes.
steps.helpDetail: Expect vomiting—turn head to side to clear, then continue. Do NOT press abdomen to drain water.

steps.instruction: Clear area around the person of hard or sharp objects.
steps.helpDetail: Move furniture, rocks, anything that could cause injury. Do NOT restrain the person or hold them down.

steps.instruction: Place something soft under their head.
steps.helpDetail: Jacket, towel, backpack—anything to prevent head injury from repeated impact.

steps.instruction: Do NOT put anything in their mouth.
steps.helpDetail: The swallowing-tongue myth is false. Objects cause broken teeth and choking.

steps.instruction: Time the seizure. Over 5 minutes is a medical emergency.
steps.helpDetail: After the seizure stops, place in recovery position. Stay with them—they will be confused.

techniques.name: Poison Ingestion Response
techniques.subtitle: When someone eats or drinks something toxic

steps.instruction: Identify what was ingested, how much, and when.
steps.helpDetail: Save container, plant sample, or vomit for identification.

steps.instruction: Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically directed.
steps.helpDetail: Caustic substances cause more damage returning. Petroleum products can be aspirated into lungs.

steps.instruction: If conscious, rinse mouth with water. Small sips only.
steps.helpDetail: Water dilutes the toxin. Do not give large amounts. If unconscious, do not give fluids.

steps.instruction: Monitor breathing. Place unconscious person in recovery position.
steps.helpDetail: Some poisons cause respiratory failure. Monitor constantly.

techniques.name: Stopping a Nosebleed
techniques.subtitle: Lean forward, pinch soft part of nose

steps.instruction: Sit upright and lean FORWARD slightly.
steps.helpDetail: Leaning back causes blood to drain down throat. Forward lets blood drain out.

steps.instruction: Pinch the soft part of the nose firmly for 10-15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Just below the bony bridge. Constant pressure—do not check early.

steps.instruction: Apply cold compress to bridge of nose.
steps.helpDetail: Cold constricts blood vessels. Ice wrapped in cloth or cold water on bandana.

steps.instruction: If bleeding persists beyond 20 minutes, seek help.
steps.helpDetail: May indicate posterior bleed requiring medical intervention.

steps.instruction: Do NOT remove the impaled object.
steps.helpDetail: The object may be plugging a blood vessel. Removing can cause fatal hemorrhage.

steps.instruction: Stabilize the object so it cannot move.
steps.helpDetail: Build padding around the object on all sides. Tape or tie padding in place.

steps.instruction: Control bleeding around the object with direct pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Press firmly AROUND the object, not on it.

steps.instruction: Immobilize the body part and transport carefully.
steps.helpDetail: If object is long, shorten by cutting—never pull or push.

techniques.name: Diabetic Emergency
techniques.subtitle: Low blood sugar can kill in minutes

steps.instruction: Recognize: confusion, sweating, shaking, pale skin, irritability.
steps.helpDetail: Low blood sugar mimics intoxication. If someone with diabetes acts confused, assume sugar emergency.

steps.instruction: If conscious: give sugar immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Fruit juice, regular soda, candy, honey. Fast-acting sugar, 15-20 grams.

steps.instruction: Wait 15 minutes. If not improving, give more sugar.
steps.helpDetail: Repeat if symptoms persist. Most improve within 10-15 minutes.

steps.instruction: If unconscious: recovery position, do NOT give food or drink.
steps.helpDetail: Unconscious people choke. Place in recovery position. Check for medical alert bracelet.

steps.instruction: If crushed less than 15 minutes, remove the weight immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Short compression is safe to release. Apply tourniquet BEFORE releasing if limb was crushed longer.

steps.instruction: If crushed more than 15 minutes: apply tourniquet BEFORE releasing.
steps.helpDetail: Crush syndrome releases toxins from dead muscle into blood, causing cardiac arrest. Tourniquet prevents this.

steps.instruction: Give fluids aggressively if victim is conscious.
steps.helpDetail: IV fluids are ideal. Oral water is better than nothing. Hydration dilutes toxins.

steps.instruction: Monitor for shock and cardiac symptoms after release.
steps.helpDetail: Irregular heartbeat, muscle rigidity, dark urine. These indicate crush syndrome. Medical emergency.

steps.instruction: Do NOT touch the victim until power source is disconnected.
steps.helpDetail: Electricity travels through bodies. You will become a second victim. Turn off breaker, unplug, or use dry wooden stick to separate.

steps.instruction: Once safe, check breathing and pulse immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Electricity disrupts heart rhythm. Be prepared for CPR. Lightning strike victims have best survival rates with immediate CPR.

steps.instruction: Treat entry and exit burns.
steps.helpDetail: Electrical burns have small surface wounds but massive internal damage. Cover with sterile dressing. Do not apply ice.

steps.instruction: Monitor for 24 hours — delayed cardiac arrhythmia is common.
steps.helpDetail: Even if the person seems fine, electrical injury can cause delayed heart problems. Keep monitoring.

techniques.name: Heart Attack Recognition
techniques.subtitle: Chest pain, jaw pain, arm pain — act fast

steps.instruction: Recognize: crushing chest pressure, left arm/jaw pain, shortness of breath, cold sweat.
steps.helpDetail: Women may have atypical symptoms: nausea, back pain, fatigue.

steps.instruction: Sit person upright, knees bent, back supported.
steps.helpDetail: Sitting reduces heart workload. Do not let them lie flat or exert.

steps.instruction: Give aspirin if available — have them chew it.
steps.helpDetail: Chewing gets it into bloodstream faster. 325mg. Thins blood.

steps.instruction: If unresponsive, begin CPR immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Heart attacks frequently lead to cardiac arrest. Be prepared.

techniques.name: Stroke Recognition (FAST)
techniques.subtitle: Face, Arm, Speech, Time — act immediately

steps.instruction: FACE: Ask them to smile. Does one side droop?
steps.helpDetail: Facial drooping on one side is a hallmark stroke sign.

steps.instruction: ARMS: Raise both arms. Does one drift down?
steps.helpDetail: One-sided weakness indicates stroke.

steps.instruction: SPEECH: Repeat a sentence. Is it slurred?
steps.helpDetail: Garbled, slurred, or confused speech is a stroke sign.

steps.instruction: TIME: Note when symptoms started. Get help NOW.
steps.helpDetail: Every minute kills 1.9 million brain cells. Time is brain.

techniques.name: Asthma Attack Response
techniques.subtitle: Sit upright, slow breathing, use inhaler

steps.instruction: Sit upright, lean slightly forward, hands on knees.
steps.helpDetail: Tripod position opens airways. Never lay them down.

steps.instruction: Help use inhaler: shake, exhale fully, inhale while pressing.
steps.helpDetail: 2 puffs, 30-60 sec between. No inhaler: pursed lip breathing.

steps.instruction: Coach slow breathing: 4 in through nose, 6 out pursed lips.
steps.helpDetail: Panic worsens bronchospasm. Speak calmly.

steps.instruction: Blue lips or inability to speak = emergency.
steps.helpDetail: Blue lips = oxygen deprivation. Cannot speak in full sentences = severe.

techniques.name: Infant Choking Response
techniques.subtitle: Back blows and chest thrusts for babies

steps.instruction: Place infant face-down on forearm, head lower than chest.
steps.helpDetail: Support head and jaw. Forearm rests on thigh.

steps.instruction: 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades.
steps.helpDetail: Sharp deliberate blows. Angle and gravity help dislodge.

steps.instruction: Flip face-up. 5 chest thrusts with two fingers on breastbone.
steps.helpDetail: Below nipple line. Push 1.5 inches. Sharp thrusts.

steps.instruction: Repeat until expelled. If unconscious, begin infant CPR.
steps.helpDetail: Check mouth for visible object before each breath.

techniques.name: Dislocated Joint Management
techniques.subtitle: Immobilize in position found — do not relocate

steps.instruction: Do NOT try to pop the joint back in.
steps.helpDetail: Field reduction risks nerve, vessel, and bone damage.

steps.instruction: Immobilize in current position with splint or sling.
steps.helpDetail: Stabilize above and below the joint.

steps.instruction: Apply cold compress. 20 minutes on, 20 off.
steps.helpDetail: Reduces swelling and pain.

steps.instruction: Check circulation below injury: pulse, sensation, color.
steps.helpDetail: Cold, numb, or blue = compressed blood vessels. Urgent.

techniques.name: Hyperventilation Management
techniques.subtitle: Coach slow breathing — no paper bag

steps.instruction: Reassure: they are NOT dying. It feels like it, but they are safe.
steps.helpDetail: Hyperventilation causes tingling, chest tightness, dizziness.

steps.instruction: Coach: 4 counts in through nose, 7 counts out through mouth.
steps.helpDetail: Goal is to slow breathing and increase CO2 levels.

steps.instruction: Do NOT use a paper bag — outdated and dangerous.
steps.helpDetail: Paper bag can cause oxygen depletion. Coaching is safer.

steps.instruction: If not anxious, check for other causes (DKA, asthma, heart).
steps.helpDetail: Non-anxiety hyperventilation is a medical emergency.

techniques.name: Emergency Childbirth
techniques.subtitle: Delivery when help is not coming

steps.instruction: Prepare a clean area. Wash hands and groin area.
steps.helpDetail: Sterility is key. Boil water if possible. Use clean cloths.

steps.instruction: Support the head as it crowns. Do NOT pull.
steps.helpDetail: Apply gentle counter-pressure to prevent tearing. Let the baby emerge naturally.

steps.instruction: Clear baby's airway. Dry and warm immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Wipe mouth and nose. Skin-to-skin contact with mother for warmth.

steps.instruction: Do not cut cord until it stops pulsing (1-3 mins).
steps.helpDetail: Tie with sterile string in two places, cut between. Deliver placenta naturally — do not pull.

articles.title: Controlling Bleeding
articles.body:
Blood loss kills faster than almost any other trauma. A person can bleed to death from a severed femoral artery in 3-5 minutes.

Bleeding control priority:
1. Direct pressure — your hands and a cloth on the wound
2. Pressure bandage — wrap tightly (Israeli/emergency bandage)
3. Tourniquet — for life-threatening limb bleeding only
4. Wound packing — for junctional areas (groin, armpit, neck)

Tourniquet myths debunked: Modern evidence shows tourniquets can stay on for 6+ hours without limb loss. The old 'you'll lose the limb' teaching is wrong. A loose tourniquet is more dangerous than a tight one — it blocks venous return while allowing arterial flow, increasing bleeding.

Wound packing: For deep wounds that won't stop bleeding, pack gauze INTO the wound, pressing firmly against the bleeding vessel. This feels wrong but saves lives.

articles.title: CPR: What You Need to Know
articles.body:
Compression-only CPR (hands-only) is nearly as effective as full CPR for adults who collapse suddenly. If you're unwilling or unable to give mouth-to-mouth, compressions alone still save lives.

Key numbers:
- 100-120 compressions per minute (beat of 'Stayin' Alive')
- 2 inches deep minimum for adults
- Full chest recoil between compressions
- 30 compressions : 2 breaths ratio

Quality degrades rapidly. Switch rescuers every 2 minutes. Most people push too shallow after 1 minute of fatigue.

For children: same ratio but use one hand. For infants: two fingers, 1.5 inches deep.

AED (Automated External Defibrillator): If available, use immediately. They are designed for untrained users. Open the case and follow voice prompts. You cannot hurt someone with an AED — it only shocks if needed.

articles.title: Fracture Recognition & Management
articles.body:
Assume a fracture exists if there is:
- Deformity (limb looks wrong)
- Swelling at a specific point
- Crepitus (grinding sensation)
- Loss of function
- Point tenderness (pain at one specific spot when pressed)

Open fracture (bone visible through skin): This is a surgical emergency. Cover the wound with a moist sterile dressing. Do NOT push bone back in. Splint and evacuate.

Femur fractures are life-threatening — the thigh can hold 1-2 liters of internal blood. Watch for shock.

General splinting rules:
- Splint in position found
- Immobilize joint above AND below the break
- Pad all bony prominences
- Check circulation every 15 minutes
- Never straighten a deformity in the field

articles.title: Burn Classification & Treatment
articles.body:
Burns are classified by depth:

1st degree (superficial): Red, painful, no blisters. Like a sunburn. Treat with cool water and aloe vera. Heals in 3-5 days.

2nd degree (partial thickness): Red, painful, blisters present. Very painful because nerve endings are intact but exposed. Cool water 20 min, do not pop blisters, clean bandage. Heals in 2-3 weeks.

3rd degree (full thickness): White/brown/black, often painless (nerves are destroyed). Requires skin grafting. Do not cool — wrap and evacuate.

The Rule of Nines for estimating burn area:
- Head: 9%
- Each arm: 9%
- Chest front: 18%
- Back: 18%
- Each leg: 18%
- Groin: 1%

Burns over 20% body surface area require IV fluid resuscitation. This is a hospital emergency.

articles.title: Venomous Bites & Stings
articles.body:
Snake bites: Only 20% of bites from venomous snakes actually envenomate (dry bites are common). But ALWAYS treat as envenomated until proven otherwise. The only treatment is antivenom at a hospital. Everything else (cutting, sucking, tourniquet, electric shock) makes it worse.

Spider bites: Brown recluse causes tissue necrosis — clean, ice, and evacuate. Black widow causes muscle cramps and pain — seek medical care but rarely fatal in adults.

Scorpion stings: Most are painful but not dangerous. Exception: Bark scorpion (southwestern US) can cause neurological symptoms in children. Seek medical care.

Tick-borne diseases: Lyme disease (bull's-eye rash), Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (spotted rash on palms/soles), and many others. Early antibiotic treatment is critical.

articles.title: Environmental Emergencies
articles.body:
Hypothermia stages:
- Mild (95-89°F): Shivering, confusion, poor coordination
- Moderate (89-82°F): Shivering stops, severe confusion, drowsiness
- Severe (<82°F): Unconscious, very slow pulse, appears dead

The phrase 'nobody is dead until they're warm and dead' exists because severely hypothermic patients have survived with no detectable pulse. Continue CPR and rewarm.

Heat illness spectrum:
Heat cramps → Heat exhaustion → Heat stroke

The critical difference: heat exhaustion patients are sweating. Heat stroke patients have stopped sweating. Heat stroke = emergency department NOW.

Drowning: Start CPR immediately. 5 rescue breaths first (water in lungs prevents oxygen exchange). Do not attempt to drain water. Begin standard CPR protocol. People have survived 30+ minutes of submersion in cold water.

Lightning: Lightning victims do NOT carry a charge. They are safe to touch. Start CPR immediately. Lightning strikes have high survival rates with prompt CPR.

articles.title: Anaphylaxis & Allergic Reactions
articles.body:
Anaphylaxis is a whole-body allergic reaction that can kill in minutes. Common triggers: bee stings, peanuts, shellfish, medications, latex.

Symptom progression:
1. Skin: hives, flushing, itching
2. Swelling: face, lips, tongue, throat
3. Respiratory: wheezing, stridor, shortness of breath
4. Cardiovascular: rapid pulse, low blood pressure, dizziness
5. GI: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea

Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line treatment. Antihistamines (Benadryl) are too slow to save a life. EpiPen goes in the outer thigh, through clothing is fine.

Biphasic reaction: Up to 20% of anaphylaxis patients have a second wave 1-72 hours later. Hospital observation for at least 4 hours is essential.

articles.title: Survival First Aid Kit Essentials
articles.body:
Minimum wilderness first aid kit:

Bleeding control:
- Tourniquet (CAT or SOFTT-W)
- Israeli bandage
- Hemostatic gauze (QuikClot)
- Roller gauze (2x)
- Medical tape

Wound care:
- Irrigation syringe
- Wound closure strips (Steri-Strips)
- Tincture of benzoin
- Antibiotic ointment
- Non-stick pads

Medications:
- Ibuprofen 200mg
- Diphenhydramine 25mg (Benadryl)
- Loperamide (Imodium)
- Electrolyte packets
- Epinephrine auto-injector (if prescribed)

Tools:
- Trauma shears
- Fine-point tweezers
- Safety pins
- Nitrile gloves (4+ pairs)
- SAM splint
- Moleskin
- Elastic bandage (ACE wrap)
- Emergency blanket

All items should be checked and refreshed annually. Medications expire. Adhesives degrade.

articles.title: Triage & Treatment Priorities
articles.body:
When someone is injured, assess in this order (MARCH):

M — Massive hemorrhage: Stop life-threatening bleeding FIRST. This kills fastest.

A — Airway: Is the airway open? Head tilt, chin lift. Clear obstructions.

R — Respiration: Are they breathing? Look, listen, feel. Start rescue breathing if not.

C — Circulation: Is there a pulse? Start CPR if not. Treat for shock.

H — Hypothermia/Head injury: Prevent heat loss. Assess level of consciousness.

The biggest mistake in field medicine is treating the dramatic wound while missing the critical one. A tourniquet on a spurting artery takes priority over a broken arm.

Scene safety: ALWAYS ensure the scene is safe before providing care. You cannot help if you become a victim. Check for traffic, fire, electrical hazards, aggressive animals, or ongoing violence.
